title: Dog Scratching Ear Until It Bleeds: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog scratching their ear to the point of bleeding? Discover common causes, urgency levels, and how to help your pet get relief quickly.
content_html:
What Is Causing My Dog to Scratch Until They Bleed?

When a dog scratches their ear aggressively enough to cause bleeding or self-trauma, it is usually a sign of intense discomfort or pain. This behavior is often triggered by an underlying issue such as a yeast or bacterial ear infection, ear mites, or environmental allergies. In some cases, a foreign object like a grass seed may be trapped inside the canal, or the dog may have developed an aural hematoma—a painful blood-filled pocket in the ear flap caused by the force of shaking their head.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is categorized as Medium. While scratching until bleeding is typically not a life-threatening emergency, it indicates significant distress and physical pain. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. If the ear is swelling rapidly or if your dog is acting lethargic or losing their balance, seek care sooner to prevent further trauma to the ear canal and skin.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a clear photo of the ear can be incredibly helpful for triage. A high-quality image allows the medical team to see the extent of the inflammation, the color and consistency of any discharge, and whether there is visible swelling or an open wound. This information helps the clinic determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and allows them to prepare the necessary diagnostic tools before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Can I put anything in my dog's ear to stop the itching?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid putting over-the-counter drops or cleaners in the ear until a vet has checked the eardrum. If the eardrum is ruptured, certain liquids can cause permanent damage or deafness.

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from scratching until my vet appointment?
faqs.answer: The most effective way to prevent further self-injury is to use an Elizabethan collar (the 'cone of shame'). This prevents the hind legs from reaching the ear and allows any existing wounds to begin healing.

faqs.question: Will an ear infection go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No, canine ear infections generally require medicated drops or systemic antibiotics prescribed by a veterinarian. Left untreated, they can spread to the middle or inner ear.
